summary: German photographer (born 1955 in Leipzig), student of Bernd and Hilla Becher at the Düsseldorf School alongside Thomas Struth, Candida Höfer, and Thomas Ruff. Known for mural-sized, large-format photographs extensively digitally manipulated using Photoshop, often taken from high vantage points. His photograph Rhein II (a near-abstract 12-foot-wide image of the Rhine River between bands of grass and sky, with factories and a dog walker digitally removed) sold for $4.3 million in 2011. Also known for 99 Cent (and 99 Cent II Diptychon), the Pyongyang series, and Review (four chancellors admiring a Barnett Newman).

works.name: Rhein II (1999)
works.description: Gursky's most famous photograph — a near-abstract depiction of the Rhine River showing two horizontal bands of grass (one above, one below) separated by the gray-green river under a gray sky. Digitally edited to remove factories, a dog walker, and pedestrians from the horizon. The 12-foot-wide print was taken near Düsseldorf. Sold for $4.3 million in 2011 — the most expensive photograph ever sold at auction (until Man Ray's Le Violon d'Ingres surpassed it in 2022). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Near-abstract image of the Rhine between two horizontal bands of grass and a gray sky; sold for $4.3 million in 2011 — the most expensive photograph ever sold; factories digitally removed from the horizon
examples: This photographer showed alternating bands of gray and green in his shot of a riverbank that ranks as the most expensive photograph ever sold.

This photographer, who created the Pyongyang series, made a color print mounted to glass that depicts two horizontal patches of grass, which are both bordered by a river; that work Rhein II is the most expensive photograph ever sold.

clues.clue: The digital editing removed factories, a dog walker, and pedestrians from the horizon; the print is 12 feet wide
examples: That picture depicts this German river and is numbered 'II'; Gursky's editing removed the large factories on the horizon.

An artist in this country digitally removed many details from a photo of a river and flat green fields, a 12-foot-wide print of which sold for a record price in 2011.

clues.clue: The original photograph was taken near Düsseldorf; Man Ray's Le Violon d'Ingres surpassed it as most expensive photograph in 2022
examples: The original photograph was taken a short distance outside Dusseldorf.
Until the 2022 sale of Man Ray's Le Violon d'Ingres, the most expensive photo ever sold was a bare photo of one of these locations by Andreas Gursky.

works.name: 99 Cent and 99 Cent II Diptychon (1999, 2001)
works.description: 99 Cent (1999) shows brightly colored, neatly organized rows of commercial goods on shelves in a discount California supermarket; the title word appears on the wall next to 'thanks,' 'only,' and 'nothing over.' 99 Cent II Diptychon (2001) is a two-panel version of a similar scene with hyper-saturated fluorescent lighting. The diptych was the fourth most expensive photograph ever sold. Both are mural-sized. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows brightly colored rows of products in a discount California supermarket; title word appears on the wall next to 'thanks,' 'only,' and 'nothing over'
examples: This man took a 1999 image, depicting people browsing fluorescently lit aisles, in which the title word appears on the walls upper next to the words 'thanks,' 'only,' and 'nothing over.'
This photographer showed aisles of products at a California superstore in his 99 Cent series.

clues.clue: 99 Cent II Diptychon is a two-panel version with hyper-saturated colors; was the fourth most expensive photograph ever sold
examples: A 2001 image of a similar scene is presented as a diptych and is the fourth-most-expensive photograph ever sold.
This artist's 99 Cent II Diptychon depicts rows of colorful products in a supermarket.

works.name: Pyongyang Series (2007)
works.description: Series of photographs taken at the Arirang Festival (Mass Games) in Pyongyang, North Korea. Shows thousands of cheerleaders in matching costumes with paneled depictions of flowers and doves in the background. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows thousands of cheerleaders in matching costumes at the Arirang Festival in Pyongyang, with paneled depictions of flowers and doves in the background
examples: Paneled depictions of flowers and doves appear in the background of thousands of cheerleaders in this artist's Pyongyang series.
Another photograph by this artist depicts over a thousand women wearing cheerleading costumes in front of a panorama of flowers, and was made at the Arirang Festival.

works.name: Stock Exchange Series and Other Works
works.description: Series of photographs depicting stock exchanges in Tokyo, Chicago, Hong Kong, Kuwait, New York, and other cities from aerial/high vantage points, using camera blur to capture movement. Chicago Board of Trade II is one example. Other works include: Bahrain I (Formula One racetrack in the desert); a photograph of the Super-Kamiokande neutrino observatory (showing golden spheres/phototubes); and a photograph of an Italian beach with hundreds of umbrellas grouped in colorful sections. Wikipedia

examples: Two series of claustrophobic aerial photographs by this artist depict stock exchanges in Tokyo and Chicago.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: German photographer (born 1955 in Leipzig). Student of Bernd and Hilla Becher at the Düsseldorf School of Photography, alongside Thomas Struth, Candida Höfer, and Thomas Ruff. Known for large-format, mural-sized photographs extensively digitally edited (using Photoshop). Photographs often taken from high vantage points showing repetitive patterns and crowds. Michael Fried discusses Gursky in his 2008 book Why Photography Matters As Art As Never Before. Other works include Review (a manipulated image of four German chancellors admiring a Barnett Newman painting), Politik II (showing 13 politicians in conversation under a clock from an Ed Ruscha painting), Shanghai (depicting a hotel), a hotel interior combining three-floor shots into a rippling yellow light pattern, and a photograph of an Italian beach. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Andreas Gursky (born 1955 in Leipzig) is a German photographer and student of Bernd and Hilla Becher at the Düsseldorf School of Photography, alongside fellow students Thomas Struth, Candida Höfer, and Thomas Ruff. He is known for large-format, mural-sized photographs extensively digitally manipulated using Photoshop to create repetitive patterns, surreal compositions, and expanded perspectives, often taken from high vantage points. Michael Fried discusses Gursky in his 2008 book Why Photography Matters As Art As Never Before.

His most famous work, Rhein II (1999), is a near-abstract 12-foot-wide image of the Rhine River (taken near Düsseldorf) showing two bands of green grass separated by the river under a gray sky — factories, a dog walker, and pedestrians were digitally removed from the scene. It sold for $4.3 million in 2011, the most expensive photograph ever sold at auction (until Man Ray's Le Violon d'Ingres surpassed it in 2022). Before Gursky's record, Cindy Sherman's Centerfolds series held the price record. His 99 Cent (1999) shows the brightly colored, neatly organized shelves of a California discount supermarket, with the title word appearing on the wall alongside 'thanks,' 'only,' and 'nothing over.' The two-panel 99 Cent II Diptychon (2001) is a hyper-saturated version and was the fourth most expensive photograph ever sold.

Gursky's Pyongyang series (2007) documents the Arirang Festival in North Korea, showing thousands of matching cheerleaders with paneled depictions of flowers and doves behind them. He made a series of aerial photographs of stock exchanges (Tokyo, Chicago, Hong Kong, Kuwait, New York) using blur to capture movement; Chicago Board of Trade II is one example. His Bahrain I shows the Sakhir Grand Prix racetrack as a black tangle in the desert landscape. He also photographed the Super-Kamiokande neutrino observatory, showing golden spheres (phototubes) reflected in the water with two humans on boats. Politik II depicts 13 politicians in conversation under a clock from an Ed Ruscha painting. Review is a manipulated image of four German chancellors admiring a Barnett Newman painting. Shanghai depicts a hotel.
recursive_suggestions: Bernd and Hilla Becher — Gursky's teachers; the Düsseldorf School; associated with industrial typological photography
Cindy Sherman — repeatedly appears in the same bonus parts as Gursky for most expensive photographs
Düsseldorf School — the broader movement; could search for specific members
